methodologies.name: Orchestration Pattern Selection
methodologies.summary: Before wiring agents together, classify the candidate orchestration as either Workflow (explicit control flow — sequential, conditional, parallel) or Autonomous (emergent control — plan-based, handoff, group-chat), then apply comparative criteria — predictability, latency, observability, cost, failure-mode shape — to pick one. When neither family alone fits, compose hybrids: a workflow shell around an autonomous core, or autonomous agents inside workflow stages. Skipping the classification step and reaching straight for autonomy is the default mistake that produces unobservable agent graphs.
methodologies.intent: Force a deliberate choice between explicit-workflow and autonomous-orchestration shapes against named criteria before any agent graph is committed to code.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Designing a multi-agent system from scratch, or refactoring one whose failure modes have outgrown its current orchestration shape. Apply when you can name the task but cannot yet defend the chosen topology against alternatives. Don't apply for a single-agent loop with one tool — there is no orchestration choice to make. Exceptions: when the deployment platform dictates one family (a strict workflow engine, or a swarm-only runtime), the selection collapses to a sub-choice within that family.

inputs.name: Task decomposition
inputs.description: A breakdown of the user-facing task into candidate agent responsibilities.

inputs.name: Operating constraints
inputs.description: Named constraints — latency budget, cost ceiling, observability requirements, tolerance for non-determinism.

inputs.name: Candidate patterns
inputs.description: Pattern library to draw from (sequential, conditional, parallel, plan-and-execute, handoff, group-chat, hybrid).

outputs.name: Selected orchestration pattern
outputs.description: Named primary pattern with the family — Workflow or Autonomous — declared explicitly.

outputs.name: Selection rationale
outputs.description: Written justification scoring the chosen pattern against alternatives on the comparative criteria.

outputs.name: Hybrid composition (optional)
outputs.description: If neither family alone fits, the explicit shell/core arrangement — e.g. workflow stages wrapping an autonomous sub-team.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  start[Task decomposition + constraints] --> classify[Classify candidate topologies]
  classify --> wf{Workflow family fits?}
  wf -- yes --> wfpick[Pick sequential / conditional / parallel]
  wf -- no --> auto{Autonomous family fits?}
  auto -- yes --> autopick[Pick plan-execute / handoff / group-chat]
  auto -- no --> hybrid[Compose hybrid: workflow shell over autonomous core]
  wfpick --> score[Score against predictability, latency, observability, cost]
  autopick --> score
  hybrid --> score
  score --> pick[Apply selection criteria: weakest topology that solves]
  pick --> doc[Document rejected alternatives]
  doc --> out[Selected orchestration + rationale]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A platform team at a B2B analytics company is wiring up an internal 'data question' assistant. The task: a user asks a natural-language question about their warehouse, the system retrieves the right schema slice, drafts SQL, runs it against a sandboxed replica, and returns a chart with prose. The team's instinct was 'multi-agent group chat with a planner, an analyst, and a charter agent.'

Applying the methodology, they classified that candidate as Autonomous (group-chat). Scoring against criteria, group-chat lost on predictability (the planner regularly relitigated decisions) and observability (no clean per-stage trace). A workflow alternative — sequential schema-fetch → SQL-draft → SQL-execute → chart-render — won on every criterion except the rare 'multi-warehouse join' query class. They picked the workflow as the primary topology, with a single autonomous escape hatch for joins that the planner could trigger. The composition was documented: workflow shell, autonomous sub-team only invoked for the join class.

What they learned: writing down the rejected group-chat design and the criteria it lost on stopped a junior engineer four months later from 'simplifying things by going group-chat again.' The rationale, not the topology, was the durable artefact. Per-stage P95 latency dropped from 38s to 11s once they were no longer paying for emergent coordination on every query.

steps.name: Classify candidate orchestrations into the taxonomy
steps.description: For each plausible topology, tag it Workflow (sequential, conditional, parallel) or Autonomous (plan-based, handoff, group-chat). A topology that cannot be tagged is under-specified — go back and decompose further.

steps.name: Score against comparative criteria
steps.description: Score each candidate on predictability, latency, observability, cost, and characteristic failure-mode shape. Workflow tends to win on predictability and observability; Autonomous tends to win on flexibility and emergent task coverage.

steps.name: Apply selection criteria
steps.description: Pick the weakest topology — the most constrained, most explicit shape — that still solves the task. Autonomy is paid for in observability and guardrails; only buy what the task demands.

steps.name: Compose hybrids when no single pattern fits
steps.description: If predictability and emergent coverage are both required, wrap an autonomous sub-team inside a workflow shell, or interleave workflow stages between autonomous segments. The composition itself becomes the named topology.

steps.name: Document the rejected alternatives
steps.description: Record what was considered and why it was rejected. The next team to touch the system inherits both the choice and the reasoning — without the rationale, the topology drifts back to whatever feels fashionable.

principles: Pick the most constrained orchestration that still solves the task — autonomy is paid for in observability.
Hybrids are named compositions, not undocumented escape hatches.
Every selection writes down its rejected alternatives, or the choice is unauditable.
Workflow and Autonomous are families, not values — never select 'multi-agent' without naming the pattern.

known_failure_modes.note: Reaching for the orchestrator-workers pattern by default until the orchestrator becomes a serial chokepoint for every multi-agent decision.
Selecting an autonomous topology when a workflow would suffice, then loading one agent with every responsibility.
Choosing a long sequential workflow without budgeting for multiplicative per-step error.

sources.title: Designing Multi-Agent Systems: Principles, Patterns, and Implementation for AI Agents (Victor Dibia, 2025)
sources.chapter: Ch 2 'Multi-Agent Patterns' — §2.1 A Taxonomy of Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns; §2.2 Workflow Patterns (Explicit Control); §2.3 Autonomous Patterns (Emergent Control); §2.4 Pattern Selection and Comparison

sources.title: designing-multiagent-systems (companion repository, Victor Dibia)
sources.quote: Part I: Foundations — Ch 1 Understanding Multi-Agent Systems, Ch 2 Multi-Agent Patterns, Ch 3 UX Design Principles

methodologies.name: Protocol Selection: MCP Vs A2A
methodologies.summary: Choose the wire protocol for a distributed agent deployment by mapping the integration shape: MCP (Model Context Protocol) when a host agent needs typed access to tools and data sources, A2A (Agent-to-Agent) when peer agents collaborate across organisational or trust boundaries, both together when an agent is simultaneously a tool consumer and a peer. Gate the decision on security considerations — credential surface, transport, identity propagation — rather than picking the protocol that came first in the docs.
methodologies.intent: Pick the right wire protocol — MCP, A2A, or both — for a distributed-agent integration by mapping the integration shape and security posture, not by defaulting to whichever protocol is trending.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Integrating an agent with external tools, data, or other agents across a process or organisational boundary. Apply before opening a port or publishing a manifest. Don't apply when everything lives in a single process — direct function calls are simpler and avoid the protocol tax. Exceptions: a single-process system that anticipates a near-term split may still benefit from drawing the protocol seam early.

inputs.name: Integration inventory
inputs.description: List of every external system the agent must talk to — tools, data sources, peer agents — with their owners and trust boundaries.

inputs.name: Trust map
inputs.description: Which integrations cross an organisational boundary, which carry user credentials, which are first-party.

inputs.name: Latency and throughput requirements
inputs.description: Per-call SLA and aggregate-traffic profile for each integration.

outputs.name: Per-integration protocol assignment
outputs.description: Each integration tagged MCP, A2A, both, or 'direct' (no protocol — in-process).

outputs.name: Security posture per protocol
outputs.description: Identity propagation, transport, credential scope, and audit hook for each protocol seam.

outputs.name: Composition note
outputs.description: Where MCP and A2A are used together, an explicit account of which agent plays which role at the seam.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  inv[Integration inventory + trust map] --> classify{Per integration: shape?}
  classify -- consumes tool/data --> mcp[Assign MCP]
  classify -- peer agent collab --> a2a[Assign A2A]
  classify -- in-process --> direct[Direct call, no protocol]
  classify -- both roles --> both[Assign MCP + A2A]
  mcp --> sec{Security gate: credentials, TLS, audit, identity?}
  a2a --> sec
  both --> sec
  sec -- pass --> pin[Pin protocol versions + capability manifests]
  sec -- fail --> reconsider[Reconsider protocol or harden seam]
  reconsider --> classify
  pin --> rec[Record rejected alternatives]
  rec --> out[Per-integration protocol assignment + security posture]

methodologies.example_scenario:
An engineering team at a logistics SaaS is adding an 'ops copilot' agent that talks to internal services and to a partner's pricing-optimisation agent. Their integration inventory: a Postgres warehouse, an internal route-planner API, a partner-hosted pricing agent, and a shared S3 bucket of historical shipments. Trust map: warehouse and route-planner are first-party, pricing agent crosses an organisational boundary, S3 is first-party but holds customer data.

Applying the methodology, the warehouse and route-planner became MCP servers — typed tools the copilot consumes on behalf of an authenticated user, with user identity propagated as an OAuth claim. The pricing-agent integration became A2A, with mTLS, a capability manifest pinned at v0.3, and a deliberate scope-down so the partner only sees an opaque task ID and aggregate parameters. The S3 bucket became MCP as well, but routed through a thin server that enforces a per-customer prefix. The copilot itself plays both roles: it is an MCP client for warehouse/route/S3, and an A2A peer when it offloads pricing optimisation.

What they learned: writing down the rejected option — 'expose route-planner via A2A because we already had A2A client code' — saved them six weeks later when the partner's A2A library went through a breaking change. They had a working MCP path the whole time. The security gate caught two seams where credential scope was effectively root; both got scoped down before launch.

steps.name: Map each integration to a protocol family
steps.description: If the agent consumes a tool or data source on behalf of a user, that seam wants MCP. If the agent collaborates with a peer agent — task delegation, capability discovery, opaque sub-task offload — that seam wants A2A. Some seams want both.

steps.name: Apply security considerations as a gate
steps.description: Before adopting a protocol on a given seam, name the credential scope, identity propagation, transport (TLS, mTLS), and the audit hook. A protocol that meets the functional need but cannot meet the security posture is rejected, regardless of fashion.

steps.name: Compose MCP and A2A where the agent is both consumer and peer
steps.description: An agent that exposes its capabilities to other agents (A2A) while consuming third-party tools (MCP) speaks both. Document the seam shape so the role at each port is unambiguous.

steps.name: Pin protocol versions and capability manifests
steps.description: Capture the version of each protocol in use and the capability manifest each agent or tool advertises. Upgrades become a documented event, not a silent break.

steps.name: Record the rejected alternatives
steps.description: When you pick MCP over A2A (or vice versa) for a seam, write down why. Six months later when the integration breaks, the rationale is what prevents a blind switch.

principles: MCP is for host↔tool/data; A2A is for agent↔agent — don't bend either across the other's lane.
Security posture gates the protocol choice; functional fit alone is not enough.
Composition (MCP + A2A) is normal; document the role at each seam explicitly.
Capability manifests and protocol versions are pinned artefacts, not ambient defaults.

known_failure_modes.note: Picking a protocol because it's trending without locking down transport, identity propagation, and credential scope.
Adopting MCP without curating the tool surface — every new server adds dozens of tools the model now has to disambiguate.
Picking a protocol because a single vendor's SDK is convenient, then discovering the wire format embeds vendor-specific extensions.

sources.title: Designing Multi-Agent Systems: Principles, Patterns, and Implementation for AI Agents (Victor Dibia, 2025)
sources.chapter: Ch 12 'Protocols for Distributed Agents' — §12.1 Core Concepts for Distributed Agents; §12.2 Model Context Protocol (MCP); §12.3 Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A); §12.4 Security Considerations; §12.5 Making Protocol Decisions (§12.5.1 Choosing the Right Protocol)
sources.quote: 12.1 Core Concepts for Distributed Agents ... 12.2 Model Context Protocol (MCP) ... 12.3 Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A) ... 12.4 Security Considerations ... 12.5 Making Protocol Decisions ... 12.5.1 Choosing the Right Protocol

sources.title: Model Context Protocol — specification
sources.quote: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools.

methodologies.name: Writer-Critic Iterative Loop Construction
methodologies.summary: Construct a two-agent loop where one agent generates an artefact — code, plan, summary, draft — and a separate agent evaluates it against an explicit pass/fail rubric. Iterate until the critic passes or a maximum iteration count is hit. This is the *construction process* for the structural generator-critic-separation pattern: it specifies how to wire the pair, what the rubric must contain, and how to bound the loop so it cannot run forever.
methodologies.intent: Wire a generator agent and a critic agent into an iterative loop with an explicit rubric and a hard iteration cap so quality improves through bounded review rather than single-shot generation.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When the artefact has checkable quality properties — code that must pass tests, a plan that must satisfy named constraints, a summary that must cover specified points — and single-shot generation under-performs. Apply when you can write the rubric down. Don't apply when 'good' is purely subjective and the rubric collapses into 'the user likes it' — at that point you need user-in-the-loop, not a critic agent. Exceptions: even subjective tasks benefit from a critic if you can specify the negative space (what to avoid).

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the generator must produce, including the input format and the success criteria.

inputs.name: Pass/fail rubric
inputs.description: Explicit checklist the critic applies — must be deterministic enough that two reasonable critics would agree on the verdict.

inputs.name: Maximum iteration count
inputs.description: Hard cap on the number of writer-critic rounds — typically 3 to 8 — beyond which the loop exits with the best draft so far.

outputs.name: Generator agent
outputs.description: Agent that produces the artefact and accepts critic feedback on subsequent iterations.

outputs.name: Critic agent
outputs.description: Agent that applies the rubric, emits pass/fail with structured feedback, and never silently rewrites the artefact itself.

outputs.name: Loop controller
outputs.description: The bounded driver that wires generator ↔ critic, enforces the iteration cap, and surfaces the best artefact if the loop exhausts its budget.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as Caller
  participant L as Loop controller
  participant W as Writer (generator)
  participant C as Critic (judge)
  U->>L: task spec + rubric + max_iters
  L->>W: generate(task spec)
  W-->>L: draft 1
  loop until pass or cap
    L->>C: judge(draft, rubric)
    alt pass
      C-->>L: pass
    else fail with hints
      C-->>L: fail + actionable hints
      L->>W: revise(draft, hints)
      W-->>L: next draft
    end
  end
  L-->>U: passing artefact or best-so-far

methodologies.example_scenario:
A small platform team is building an internal 'release-notes drafter' agent. It reads a merged-PR list and produces release notes the docs team will accept without rewriting. Single-shot generation gave technically correct but tonally wrong drafts that the docs team rewrote 80% of. The team decided to wire a writer-critic loop.

They authored the rubric first, before either agent existed: every entry must (1) name the user-facing capability not the internal module, (2) link to the relevant PR, (3) avoid the words 'refactor' and 'improvement' as standalone bullets, and (4) keep entries under 25 words. Verdict shape: pass / fail-with-hints / fail-terminally. Writer was Sonnet with a release-notes system prompt; Critic was a different model (GPT-4.1) with a strict rubric-only prompt that emits structured JSON. Iteration cap: 4 rounds, with the highest-rated draft kept on exhaustion.

Calibration: they ran 30 historical PR lists through the loop and had the docs lead grade results. The critic agreed with the docs lead 26/30 times; the 4 disagreements traced to two ambiguous rubric items, which they tightened. Post-launch, 70% of notes pass on iteration 2, 22% on iteration 3, and 8% hit the cap. The cap-hitters were nearly all 'huge PR batches' where the rubric was unsatisfiable in 25 words — they added a 'split batch' path. Without the bounded loop, a sycophancy bug they had earlier (critic praising on tone match) would have looped forever; the cap meant the worst case was a slightly-off draft, not an outage.

steps.name: Author the rubric the critic will apply
steps.description: Write the rubric down before either agent exists. List the dimensions to check, the verdict shape (pass/fail or scored), and what counts as 'fail with hints' versus 'fail terminally'. A rubric authored after the generator is biased by what the generator already does well.

steps.name: Build the generator agent
steps.description: Generator produces the artefact on the first turn from the task spec alone. On subsequent turns it consumes the critic's structured feedback. The generator never grades itself.

steps.name: Build the critic agent with role separation
steps.description: Critic only judges — it does not produce the artefact. Critic emits a structured verdict and, on fail, the smallest actionable hint that would let the generator improve. Same-model self-critique collapses the pair into a single agent; use a different model or at minimum a hard role-separating prompt.

steps.name: Wire the bounded loop
steps.description: Loop controller alternates generator and critic. Pass when the critic passes. Exit at iteration cap with the highest-rated artefact. Emit per-iteration trace so the loop is auditable in retrospect.

steps.name: Calibrate the rubric against ground truth
steps.description: Run a sample of artefacts through the critic and a human grader. Tighten the rubric until the critic's verdicts agree with humans at an acceptable rate. A miscalibrated critic either over-passes (false confidence) or over-fails (loop exhaustion).

steps.name: Instrument failure modes
steps.description: Log iteration counts, exit reasons, and rubric-pass rates per task class. Watch for tasks where the loop almost always exhausts the cap — that's a sign the rubric is unsatisfiable or the generator can't act on the critic's hints.

principles: Critic and generator are separate roles — same-model self-critique ceilings at the model's blind spots.
The rubric is frozen before either agent is built; rubric-after-build is contaminated by what the generator does well.
Every loop has a hard iteration cap and a 'best so far' fallback — unbounded write-critique loops are an outage waiting to happen.
Critic emits actionable hints on fail, not just verdicts — the generator must be able to revise on the next turn.

known_failure_modes.note: Generator and critic share the model and prompt family — verdicts collapse to 'the generator already thought this was good.'
No iteration cap or 'best so far' fallback — the loop runs forever when the rubric is unsatisfiable.
Critic rewards agreement with the generator's tone instead of applying the rubric — inflated pass rates.

methodologies.name: AOSE Lifecycle Methodology (Prometheus-style)
methodologies.summary: A full agent-oriented software engineering lifecycle adapted from Prometheus and related AOSE methodologies: requirements analysis, system specification, architectural design, detailed agent design, implementation, testing, and maintenance — oriented around BDI (Belief–Desire–Intention) agents. Each phase produces a named artefact that feeds the next. Distinct from lightweight 'wire up an agent loop and iterate' approaches: AOSE is heavyweight, has been used on long-lived multi-agent systems for decades, and trades agility for traceability from goal to deployed agent.
methodologies.intent: Take a multi-agent system from informal requirements to maintained production deployment through a fully traceable lifecycle, with named artefacts at each phase that survive personnel turnover.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Building a long-lived multi-agent system where traceability matters more than time-to-first-demo — safety-critical agents, regulated domains, multi-team systems where contracts between agents must be auditable, or research deployments expected to outlive the original team. Don't apply for a throwaway prototype, a single-agent loop, or a system whose requirements you expect to rewrite weekly — AOSE's overhead is wasted there. Exceptions: even lightweight projects benefit from cherry-picking AOSE artefacts (a goal hierarchy, a role model) without adopting the full lifecycle.

inputs.name: Stakeholder goals
inputs.description: Informally stated stakeholder needs — what the system should achieve, who relies on it, what success looks like.

inputs.name: Domain knowledge
inputs.description: Subject-matter expertise needed to model the environment the agents act in — entities, dynamics, constraints, percepts.

inputs.name: Available agent technology
inputs.description: BDI platform, communication infrastructure, agent toolkit, and runtime environment the implementation will target.

outputs.name: System specification
outputs.description: Goal hierarchy, scenarios, actions, percepts — the formal statement of what the system must do.

outputs.name: Architectural design
outputs.description: Agent types, role assignments, interaction protocols, and shared data.

outputs.name: Detailed agent designs
outputs.description: Per-agent capabilities, plans, beliefs, and event-handling structure.

outputs.name: Implemented multi-agent system
outputs.description: Running code that realises the architecture on the chosen BDI platform.

outputs.name: Test artefacts and maintenance plan
outputs.description: Unit and integration tests for agent behaviour, plus a documented maintenance procedure for evolving the system.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  goals[Stakeholder goals + domain knowledge] --> req[Requirements analysis]
  req --> spec[System specification: goal hierarchy, scenarios, percepts/actions]
  spec --> arch[Architectural design: agent types, roles, protocols, shared data]
  arch --> det[Detailed agent design: BDI plans, beliefs, capabilities, events]
  det --> impl[Implementation on BDI platform]
  impl --> test[Testing: unit, integration, model-check key properties]
  test --> deploy[Deployed multi-agent system]
  deploy --> maint[Maintenance: walk req-goal-role-agent-plan chain on change]
  maint -.requirement shift.-> req

methodologies.example_scenario:
A research group inside a national air-traffic authority is building a long-lived multi-agent decision-support system for runway-incursion advisories. The system must outlive the original team, undergo external audit, and be patchable a decade out. They adopt the Prometheus-style AOSE lifecycle because lightweight 'wire up an agent loop' approaches would not survive the audit trail requirement.

Requirements analysis produces a goal hierarchy rooted at 'advise controllers on incursion risk' decomposed into sub-goals for detection, prioritisation, advisory generation, and acknowledgement tracking. System specification names the percepts (radar feed, ground-vehicle GPS, voice-transcript stream) and actions (push advisory, log decision, escalate). Architectural design groups goals into four BDI agent types — Detector, Prioritiser, Advisor, Logger — with explicit FIPA-style protocols between them. Detailed design specifies each agent's plans (e.g. Advisor.draft-advisory uses a context-aware plan with a fallback recipe), beliefs (e.g. current-runway-state), and event handlers. Implementation targets the Jadex BDI platform. Testing combines unit tests for plans, integration tests for the protocols, and model-checking liveness for the escalation protocol.

What they learned: two years in, when regulators demanded a new advisory class, the maintenance discipline paid for itself — the change walked cleanly from a new requirement, through a new sub-goal, into the Prioritiser's plan library, with audit-trail artefacts at every step. A separate team in the same authority had built a similar system as a single LLM agent loop; when their regulator asked the same question, they could not show what had changed or why, and rebuilt from scratch.

steps.name: Requirements analysis
steps.description: Capture stakeholder goals, scenarios, and environment characteristics. Produce a goal hierarchy with parent goals decomposed into sub-goals down to actionable leaves.

steps.name: System specification
steps.description: Formalise the system boundary: what percepts the agents receive, what actions they can take, what scenarios link goals to action sequences. Produce a system overview diagram.

steps.name: Architectural design
steps.description: Group goals into agent types; define roles; specify the interaction protocols between agents; identify shared data and where it lives. Produce an agent acquaintance model and protocols.

steps.name: Detailed agent design
steps.description: For each agent type, design its capabilities, plans, beliefs, and event-handling. BDI-oriented: a plan is a recipe for achieving a goal in a context; beliefs model the agent's view of the world.

steps.name: Implementation
steps.description: Realise the detailed designs on the target BDI platform. Map plans to platform-specific plan bodies; map beliefs to belief-base entries; map protocols to message handlers.

steps.name: Testing
steps.description: Unit-test individual plans and capabilities. Integration-test agent interactions against the protocols. Where formal methods apply, model-check key properties (e.g. liveness of protocols, safety invariants on shared data).

steps.name: Maintenance
steps.description: When requirements shift, walk the change back up the chain — requirement → goal → role → agent → plan — and update artefacts in step. The traceability is the asset; ad-hoc patches that bypass the chain destroy it.

principles: Every phase produces a named artefact; phases without artefacts are aspirational, not done.
Traceability from stakeholder goal to deployed plan is the methodology's main deliverable.
BDI orientation: beliefs, desires (goals), and intentions (plans) are first-class — not implementation details.
Maintenance walks the chain — patching code without updating upstream artefacts destroys the traceability the methodology was bought for.

known_failure_modes.note: Lifecycle stalls in detailed design forever because the team treats the artefacts as the deliverable instead of as a path to running code.
Translating a flawed manual workflow directly into a goal hierarchy bakes the flaw into every downstream agent.
Maintenance patches the implementation without updating goals and roles — over time the deployed system stops matching its own architecture.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 15 'Agent-Oriented Software Engineering' — Michael Winikoff and Lin Padgham — §1 Introduction; §2 Agent Concepts; §3 Running Example; §4 Requirements; §5 Design; §6 Detailed Design; §7 Implementation; §8 Assurance; §9 Software Maintenance; §10 Comparing Methodologies; §11 Conclusions
sources.quote: Agent-Oriented Software Engineering — Michael Winikoff and Lin Padgham — 1 Introduction, 2 Agent Concepts, 3 Running Example, 4 Requirements, 5 Design, 6 Detailed Design, 7 Implementation, 8 Assurance, 9 Software Maintenance, 10 Comparing Methodologies, 11 Conclusions

sources.title: Prometheus: A Methodology for Developing Intelligent Agents (Padgham & Winikoff, AAMAS'02 AOSE Workshop)
sources.quote: The Prometheus methodology consists of three phases ... The system specification phase focuses on identifying the basic functionalities of the system, along with inputs (percepts), outputs (actions) and any important shared data sources. The architectural design phase uses the outputs from the previous phase to determine which agents the system will contain and how they will interact. The detailed design phase looks at the internals of each agent and how it will accomplish its tasks within the overall system.
